title: Top Five Music Documentaries and ‘Woodstock ’99: Love, Peace, and Rage’
format: Rob Harvilla joins to survey the pop-star doc and rank the genre's best, then Sean talks to Garrett Price about directing Woodstock 99: Peace, Love, and Rage.

films.title: Stop Making Sense
films.note: Rob's pick for a top-three concert film of all time: straight performance, no interview material at all.

films.title: Amazing Grace
films.note: Aretha Franklin's recording sessions, shelved for decades before release; light on backstage material but you feel the room.

films.title: Prince: Sign 'o' the Times
films.note: Grouped with the pure-performance films: Prince, no framing device.

films.title: U2: Rattle and Hum
films.note: U2 at their imperial-era peak, trying to be “the most American band of all time” despite not being American: “so beautiful and so corny at the same time.”

films.title: Life Is But a Dream
films.note: Beyoncé's first HBO doc, years before Lemonade. She talks to her laptop, but never lets the control slip.

films.title: Katy Perry: Part of Me
films.note: The image that sticks: five minutes sobbing on a bed over her collapsing marriage, then up on a hydraulic lift with the smile back on for a stadium in Brazil.

films.title: Homecoming
films.note: Beyoncé's Coachella film. Rob's pick for the best of the last 20 years.

films.title: Miss Americana
films.note: Taylor Swift's chosen vulnerable moment: finding out she didn't get a Grammy nomination.

films.title: 20 Feet from Stardom
films.note: The Oscar-friendly move: turn the camera on the backup singers instead of the star.

films.title: Searching for Sugar Man
films.note: Same prestige lane as 20 Feet from Stardom: you didn't know about this person, but you should.

films.title: The Wrecking Crew
films.note: The session musicians nobody knew by name, working in the same subculture-excavation mode the Oscars tend to reward.

films.title: Instrument
films.note: Referred to on air simply as “the Fugazi documentary.” Jem Cohen's film, with Ian MacKaye hanging upside down in a basketball hoop mid-song.

films.title: Framing Britney Spears
films.note: The Hulu/New York Times doc, part of this year's run of prestige music docs.

films.title: Tina
films.note: Criticized for leaning too hard on the Ike Turner years at the expense of the five decades of work that followed.

films.title: Billie Eilish: The World's a Little Blurry
films.note: R.J. Cutler's film. Her idea of a revelation, a relationship that ended, is deliberately, almost radically mundane for a teenager this famous.

films.title: Whitney
films.note: Well-made, but a late sexual-assault allegation makes it a much harder watch than Amy for Sean.

films.title: Mary J. Blige's My Life
films.note: Cited via a friend's review: sets out to be revelatory and doesn't quite get there.

films.title: Summer of Soul (...Or, When the Revolution Could Not Be Televised)
films.note: Questlove's Harlem Cultural Festival film, named alongside Eilish and Sparks Brothers as the year's three standout music docs so far.

films.title: The Sparks Brothers
films.note: Edgar Wright's profile of Russell and Ron Mael across roughly 25 albums. Maximalist by design, with animation, claymation, and archival footage from decades of European talk shows. Ron's deadpan, unchanging on-camera stare across 30 years is the thing both hosts keep coming back to.

segments.heading: The top five
segments.note: each host ranked five, worst to best; two picks overlapped

picks.title: Dave Chappelle's Block Party
picks.note: No. 5: Michel Gondry's film of Chappelle's 2004 Bed-Stuy block party, with Kanye West, The Roots, Erykah Badu, and a Dayton, Ohio marching band bussed in to play “Jesus Walks.”

picks.title: Madonna: Truth or Dare
picks.note: No. 4: Where Rob locates the birth of the modern pop-star documentary. Warren Beatty, watching a doctor examine Madonna's throat on camera, asking why any of this is being filmed.

picks.title: The Decline of Western Civilization
picks.note: No. 3 (shared). Penelope Spheeris on the early-80s L.A. punk scene: Black Flag, X, the Germs, the Circle Jerks.

picks.title: The Decline of Western Civilization Part II: The Metal Years
picks.note: No. 3 (shared). Same director, hair metal instead of punk. Chris Holmes of W.A.S.P. drinking vodka in a pool while his mother watches is the scene everyone remembers.

picks.title: The Last Waltz
picks.note: No. 2: Scorsese's film of The Band's farewell concert, with Dylan, Neil Young, Joni Mitchell, and the Staples Singers sitting in.

picks.title: Metallica: Some Kind of Monster
picks.note: No. 1: Joe Berlinger and Bruce Sinofsky catch the band mid-implosion, therapist on payroll, recording the record that became St. Anger.

picks.title: Dig!
picks.note: No. 5: Ondi Timoner's seven-year chronicle of the Dandy Warhols and the Brian Jonestown Massacre, and Anton Newcombe's spiral in particular.

picks.title: Amy
picks.note: No. 2: Asif Kapadia's Amy Winehouse film. Sean saw one of her first U.S. shows, at Joe's Pub in New York, before Back to Black came out.

picks.title: Don't Look Back
picks.note: No. 1: D.A. Pennebaker's Dylan portrait from 1965-66, opening with the cue-card “Subterranean Homesick Blues” sequence often cited as the first music video.

films.title: Woodstock 99: Peace, Love, and Rage
films.note: Director Garrett Price on structuring the film as a three-act, Friday-to-Sunday descent; on power dynamics (generational, racial, gender) as its throughline; and on not wanting the on-screen nudity and assault accounts to tip into exploitation.

films: The Decline of Western Civilization Part III
